experiences.intentions: Player 2: I know player 3 is my evil teammate; players 0, 1, and 4 are unknown good roles. As evil Assassin and current leader on quest 1, the optimal play is usually to avoid an obviously suspicious double-evil team and instead propose a plausible team that can gain approval while preserving cover. Sending myself with a good player lets me choose fail if the team passes, but early quest 1 failures can also create heat; still, as leader I want influence and a reasonable team. Proposing 2 with one unknown reveals little beyond normal leadership behavior and is less damaging to evil than exposing a 2+3 pairing immediately.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 2 and 3 are evil. The proposed team [0,2] includes known evil player 2 on quest 1, so as Good I should reject to avoid an immediate likely fail and avoid legitimizing 2. Rejecting also subtly resists evil without revealing Merlin more than necessary.
Player 0: I have no concrete role information yet beyond my own being Good. A round-1 quest team of two that includes me and the proposer is standard and not inherently suspicious. As a Servant, approving an information-gathering first team is usually optimal, especially since rejecting without evidence gives little value and delays progress. My vote reveals willingness to test a normal opening team, which helps Good gather quest-result info.
Player 1: No concrete role information yet from quest outcomes or voting patterns. As a good Servant in round 1 on a 2-player quest, approving a reasonable opening team is standard since there is little basis to reject and early approvals avoid giving evil easy cover through chaos. My vote reveals little beyond being willing to test an initial team, which is fine for good.
Player 2: I am Evil Assassin with teammate 3. The proposed team is [0,2], which includes me but excludes my known evil teammate, so on a 2-player quest this gives me sole control to fail if it goes. As proposer I already put this team forward, so approving is consistent and avoids drawing suspicion. Early game, getting onto the first quest is good for Evil because I can choose fail and create uncertainty; my vote reveals little since leaders usually approve their own teams.
Player 3: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, so this team contains one evil and one unknown good. On team vote as Minion, early game I generally want a plausible stance that helps evil without making the evil pair too obvious. Approving a team with my teammate on it gives evil a chance to fail quest 1, and early approvals of leader's own team are common. My vote itself is private in aggregate and approving does not reveal much while maximizing evil's chance to take the first quest.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am a Good Servant on the quest team, so I must vote pass. From votes alone, player 4 rejected while others approved, but that is very weak information this early. Optimal good play on quest 1 is to pass to secure success and avoid falsely signaling evil alignment. Voting pass reveals nothing harmful and is fully consistent with my role.
Player 2: I know player 3 is my evil teammate; others are unknown. This is quest 1 with a 2-player team [0,2], and as evil on the team I can choose pass or fail. Optimal evil play in 5-player Avalon is often to fail early to secure a point and create suspicion on the other teammate, especially since no Merlin info is at stake yet. My fail reveals that at least one of 0 or 2 is evil, which implicates me somewhat because I proposed the team, but an early evil point is usually worth it.

experiences.intentions: Player 3: Player 2 is confirmed evil to me, and the last failed 2-person quest [2,0] had exactly 1 fail, which strongly suggests 0 is likely good if 2 used the fail. As evil leader on a 3-person team, I want a composition likely to pass approval while preserving hidden info and giving evil a good chance to fail again. Including both known evils 2 and 3 risks suspicion if the quest fails and may be rejected by others; a mixed team with one evil is safer. Proposing 0,1,3 avoids the obvious double-evil pairing, is plausibly acceptable, and still gives me control to fail the quest if sent.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Merlin and know players 2 and 3 are evil. The proposed team [0,1,3] includes known evil player 3, so this team is extremely likely to fail and should be rejected. As Merlin, rejecting clearly bad teams helps good while not revealing too much, since any good player should also be suspicious of a team containing someone from the failed first mission.
Player 1: From quest 1, team [2,0] had exactly one fail, so at least one of 0 or 2 is evil. Proposed team [0,1,3] includes 0 but excludes 2, giving some chance to isolate the culprit. As a Good Servant, approving a team that tests one suspect alongside two fresh players is reasonable, especially early in quest 2. My vote reveals willingness to gather information rather than blanket rejecting all teams with 0.
Player 2: I am evil Assassin with teammate 3. Quest 1 failed on team [2,0], so from my perspective 0 is likely seen by others as suspicious because I was on it and caused/participated in the fail. Proposed team [0,1,3] includes my evil teammate 3 and excludes me, which is strong for evil because 3 can fail quest 2. Approving may look somewhat suspicious since it contains 0 from failed team, but evil wants this team through. The information revealed by my approve is limited and worth the chance to secure another fail.
Player 0: Quest 1 team was [2,0] and it failed with 1 fail. Since I am Good, player 2 must be Evil. That gives strong information: avoid teams including 2. Proposed team [0,1,3] excludes confirmed evil 2 and keeps me plus two unknowns, which is a strong candidate for a clean 3-person team. As a Good servant in team voting, optimal play is to approve teams that exclude confirmed evil and can generate info/success. Approving reveals I support a sensible anti-2 team, which helps Good.
Player 3: I know Player 2 is my evil teammate. The first failed quest team was [2,0], so from my perspective 0 is plausibly good and publicly somewhat suspicious, while 2 may also be under scrutiny. Current proposed team [0,1,3] excludes my teammate 2 but includes me, giving evil a guaranteed fail option if approved. As Minion on a 3-person quest, the optimal play is usually to approve teams containing me or my teammate so evil can fail; rejecting would only make it harder to get an evil player onto the quest. Approving also looks somewhat natural since I proposed this team myself, and the information revealed mainly helps evil because I can choose fail on quest.

experiences.intentions:
Player 1: Player 0 has been on both failed quests, so 0 is highly suspicious. On a 2-player quest, one fail means at least one of {2,0} is evil; with 0 also on the second failed quest, 0 is the strongest evil candidate. Team [0,4] includes the most suspicious player, so as Good I should reject to avoid likely another fail. My vote signals suspicion on 0 and helps Good coordinate away from bad teams.
Player 4: I know 2 and 3 are evil. Quest 1 team [2,0] failed with exactly 1 fail, strongly implicating 2 and making 0 likely good. Quest 2 team [0,1,3] failed with exactly 1 fail, confirming 3 as the source and making 1 also likely good. As Merlin on team voting at 0-2, I should reject any team that is likely to fail. Proposed team [0,4] excludes known evil players and is the best available 2-person team, so approving is optimal. Approving also aligns with a good player's incentives and does not leak extra Merlin-specific information beyond supporting a clearly good team.
Player 0: I know I am Good, and both prior failed quests included me, which means at least one of the other members on each team was Evil: on [2,0], player 2 must be Evil; on [0,1,3], at least one of 1 or 3 is Evil. Player 4 is the only player not implicated yet, but a team of [0,4] for a 2-person quest after two fails is too thin and could easily be 1 Good + 1 Evil. As Good in team voting, I should reject teams that do not strongly address known suspicions and do not maximize chances of a clean quest. Rejecting also signals that this proposal is not sufficiently justified.
Player 2: I know player 3 is evil with me. Quest 1 team [2,0] had exactly one fail, which could be me or 0; Quest 2 team [0,1,3] had exactly one fail, which could be 3 or possibly frame 0/1, but since 3 is evil and likely failed, 0 and 1 look somewhat better to others. Current phase is team voting on [0,4], a 2-player team without any known evil. As evil and already up 2-0, optimal play is to reject good-looking teams and push toward including me or 3 later, or toward 5 rejections if possible. My vote reveals little since evil can plausibly reject a small team after two fails.
Player 3: I know Player 2 is evil with me. Quest 1 team [2,0] failed, so 0 is likely good or at least not confirmed evil because 2 could have supplied the sole fail. Quest 2 team [0,1,3] failed, and since I am evil and would have failed, that does not strongly implicate 0 or 1. Current phase is team voting; as evil up 2-0, the optimal play is to approve a 2-player team [0,4] containing no known evil, since good teams can still fail only if members are evil, but voting patterns matter less than preserving ambiguity. Rejecting a clean-looking team could be suspicious; approving keeps my cover and if it passes, likely succeeds, but evil still has strong position and can aim to control later rounds. My vote reveals little individually in a 5-player game, and approving aligns with plausible good reasoning because 0 has been on both failed quests and 4 is untested.
